chapters.title: The Longed-For Son
chapters.text:
In a time long past, on the wide green steppe, there lived a rich man — a bai — with herds beyond counting and no child at all. He tucked up the hem of his robe and wandered the whole world over, begging heaven for a son, until at last one was granted to him.
How he loved that boy! He gave him a knucklebone of pure gold — an altyn saqa — to play with, and of all his toys the boy loved it best, and carried it wherever he went.
The Rich Man: “Keep it close, my son. There is not another like it on all the steppe — a knucklebone of gold, for the child I waited a lifetime to hold.”
The Boy: “I will never lose it, Father. When I win at knucklebones, I will win with this one!”

chapters.title: The Swallow's Debt
chapters.text:
That winter the family kept to their winter camp, the qystau, where swallows had built their mud nests under the eaves. Other children robbed such nests — but not this boy.
The Boy: “Hush, little ones. No one will harm you while I am here. Grow strong, and when spring comes I will set you free.”
The Swallow: “You are gentle where others are cruel, boy. A swallow forgets nothing. If ever you are in need, only call, and I will come.”
He tended the nestlings all the cold months long, and when the warm winds returned he loosed them into the sky. And so the boy and the swallows became friends — a small kindness that one day would save his life.

chapters.title: The Lung in the Lake
chapters.text:
One day the bai drove his horse herd down to the lake to water them. But on the still water floated a strange, bright-red lung, and the horses shied back and would not drink, however hard he drove them.
So he hooked the floating lung with his long lasso-pole and dragged it to the shore. It sank beneath the water — and rose again as a Jalmauyz Kempir, a devouring old witch, who lunged from the lake and seized him by the collar. His eyes went wide as saucers with terror.
The Rich Man: “Spare me! I will give you gold the size of a horse's head — only let me go!”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Gold? I have no use for gold. It will not buy your neck, rich man.”
The Rich Man: “Then take my whole herd of horses — every one — and let me live!”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Give me your only son, and I will let you go. Nothing less.”
The Rich Man: “...My son. So be it. Tomorrow I break camp. I will leave his golden knucklebone behind on the old ground — wait for him there, beside it.”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Beside the golden saqa. I will be waiting. See that you do not forget your promise, bai.”
So the bai gave the witch his word, and drove his horses home with a stone where his heart had been.

chapters.title: The Forgotten Knucklebone
chapters.text:
Next morning, as the aul moved to fresh pastures, the bai made certain his son's golden knucklebone was forgotten and left behind on the old campsite. But when the boy went to play, his dearest treasure was gone, and he wept for it.
The Boy: “My golden saqa — it's gone! I have looked everywhere. How can I play at knucklebones without it?”
The Rich Man: “It was left behind on the old camp when we moved, my son. Go, then... go and fetch it back.”
The Boy: “Then I will go at once! Father — what colt shall I ride to carry me there?”
The Rich Man: “Ask the horse-herder. He will know which of the herd is yours.”

chapters.title: The Mangy Colt
chapters.text:
Out on the open steppe the horse-herder watched over the bai's great herd. The boy came to him and asked for a mount for his journey.
The Horse-Herder: “Swing your lasso-pole and rattle your bridle as you walk among the horses. Whichever colt turns its face to meet you — that one is yours. Ride it, and no other.”
The Boy: “Swing the pole, rattle the bridle, ride the one that faces me. I will do exactly as you say.”
So the boy swung his pole and rattled his bridle and walked in among the herd — and out at the very edge, a scabby, mangy little colt turned and looked him full in the face.
He caught the wretched colt and bridled it — and the instant the bridle touched it, its mange healed clean away. He laid on the saddle-blanket, and it became a two-year-old; he set on the saddle, and it became a three-year-old; he tightened the girth, and it stood a full five-year-old steed. Overjoyed, the boy vaulted onto its back — and it became a tulpar, a winged steed of legend, and in the blink of an eye it bore him to the old campsite.

chapters.title: The Old Campground
chapters.text:
At the old campground his golden knucklebone lay by the cold hearth, beneath the dried dung. And beside it sat a bent old woman — the Jalmauyz Kempir, waiting, just as she had promised the bai.
The Boy: “Grandmother, be kind — hand me that knucklebone lying there beside you.”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Pick it up yourself, dear child. I am so very old — if I sit I cannot rise, and if I rise I cannot sit.”
The Boy: “And I am so very young, grandmother — if I climb down I cannot mount again, and if I mount I cannot climb down.”
Then the clever colt lay down of its own accord beside the knucklebone, and without ever drawing his foot from the stirrup the boy scooped up his golden saqa. The colt sprang up and galloped — and the crone leapt to her feet and gave chase.
Back and forth she chased him, and when she could not catch the colt she tore out one of her iron teeth and hurled it, and it struck off one of the colt's legs. On three legs it ran on. She tore out a second tooth and flung it, and struck off another leg. On two legs the colt ran, staggering — until, on its way, it came upon a great lone poplar.

chapters.title: The Great Poplar
chapters.text:
The colt was failing. As it neared the towering poplar the boy leapt from its back and climbed to the very top. Below, the Jalmauyz Kempir pulled out another tooth and began to dig at the root of the tree, and she dug on until nightfall.
The Red Fox: “Mother, you must be worn out! Lie down and rest a while — let me dig in your place.”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Kind of you, little fox. Yes... my old bones ache. Dig well, and wake me when the tree is ready to fall.”
But the moment she slept, the red fox flung her tooth into the water, filled the pit back in, and slipped away. When the crone woke, her tooth was gone and so was the fox. Snarling, she tore out another tooth, made a mattock of it, and dug on.
The White Fox: “Mother, lend me that mattock — you are tired, let me take a turn at the digging.”
Jalmauyz Kempir: “Will you take my mattock and run off, like that other thieving fox?”
The White Fox: “That was the red fox of the uplands. I am the white fox of the ravine. Long ago this boy's father hunted me and all but killed me — so I too have a score to settle with the child.”
The crone believed the sly white fox and lay down to sleep — and again the fox threw the mattock into the water, filled the pit, and fled. 'Oh, woe — tricked again!' she cried when she woke. Only one tooth was left in her mouth now, yet she dug on, and the great poplar began to lean, ready to fall.
The Boy: “Oh crows, crows, gliding on your wings — carry word to my five hounds at the summer pasture! Tell them to come and save me from the Jalmauyz Kempir!”
But the crows only cawed and flew on; and when ducks passed overhead, they too flew by in silence. Then a single swallow came flitting above the poplar and lit upon the boy's head — the very swallow he had saved.
The Boy: “Dear swallow — you told me once that you would come if ever I called. The tree is almost down. Fly to my five hounds, I beg you!”
The Swallow: “I have not forgotten your kindness, boy. Hold fast — I go now, swift as the wind!”

chapters.title: The Five Hounds
chapters.text:
The swallow flew like an arrow to the summer pasture, where the boy's five great hounds lay guarding the flocks, and cried out her message.
The Swallow: “Töbet! Far off at the lone poplar, your boy sits trapped at the very top — the Jalmauyz Kempir has dug away the root, and the tree is about to fall! Run — run now!”
The Five Hounds: “Then we run! Brothers — to the poplar, and let no one stand in our way!”
The five hounds ran, and ran, and reached the poplar just as it came crashing to the ground. They fell upon the Jalmauyz Kempir all together, and tore the devouring crone apart, and killed her.
Then the boy climbed down and rode home upon the backs of his hounds, each in turn, and came at last to his people safe and sound. His father and mother wept for joy to see him free of the witch, and they gathered all the aul and held a great feast — a toi that lasted days.

chapters.title: The Crone's Daughter
chapters.text:
But some days later, the daughter of the Jalmauyz Kempir came to the fallen poplar, and there she found her mother lying dead, her fur torn away. To learn who had done it she searched the ground, and saw the tracks of dogs.
She followed the trail across the steppe, past a great herd of horses, and straight on to the bai's camp at nightfall. The five hounds sensed her coming and sprang up together and rushed at her — but she was not like the old crone. She was terribly strong, and the five great töbet could not bring her down.
The Crone's Daughter: “You killed my mother, boy — you and your hounds! Not all five will save you from me. I have come for your life!”
The Boy: “Then it ends here, and my people will sleep safe. Steady, hounds — hold her one moment more!”
In that moment the boy drew the little bow he never rode without, and loosed an arrow that put out the daughter's eye; and with a second arrow, straight and true, he killed her. And after that the boy and all his people lived in peace on the wide green steppe.

chapters.title: Safe and Sound
chapters.text: A golden knucklebone, a father's terrible promise, and a witch risen from a lake — and against them all, a boy's kind heart and quick wits. Because he had once been gentle to a swallow's chicks, a swallow flew to fetch his hounds; because he had raised five fearless töbet, they tore the crone apart; and because he never rode without his little bow, two arrows ended her daughter's revenge. So the boy came home safe and sound, and his people lived in peace on the wide green steppe. And that is the tale of the Golden Knucklebone.
